metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the GITHUB_TOKEN permissions gap for the Rulesets API: either add `permissions: administration: read` to the workflow (if the repo allows Actions to request it) or add a stored PAT/GitHub App token with administration scope to the workflow secrets — and update the 'no new secret' claim in the Design section accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'no new secret' because 'native GITHUB_TOKEN authenticates the GitHub Rulesets API fetch', but the GitHub Rulesets API (GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets/{id}) requires the `administration` permission scope. The default GITHUB_TOKEN in GitHub Actions carries only `metadata:read` unless the workflow explicitly grants `permissions: administration: read`. Without this grant the API call returns HTTP 403, the ruleset fetch silently fails, and the drift check never runs — yet the workflow is designed to exit non-zero only when unhealthy, not when the API call itself fails.
findings.location: Design section ('native GITHUB_TOKEN authenticates the GitHub Rulesets API fetch') / AC8 (.github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml) / AC2 (ruleset_verdict)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `permissions:\n  administration: read` to the mirror-guard.yml workflow job; verify this is permitted by the repo's Actions token permissions policy (Settings → Actions → Workflow permissions). If the org restricts token permissions below this scope, document that a stored secret PAT is required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether the default GITHUB_TOKEN in Actions can read the full ruleset payload (including bypass_actors) via `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets/{id}`, and if `administration: read` permission is required, add `permissions: administration: read` to the workflow definition in AC8.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'native GITHUB_TOKEN authenticates the GitHub Rulesets API fetch' as a verified fact, but the GitHub Repository Rulesets API (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets/{id}`) requires the `administration:read` permission scope, which the default GITHUB_TOKEN in Actions does NOT include — it must be explicitly granted via `permissions: administration: read` in the workflow definition. This capability assertion is unverified in the plan.
findings.location: Design section ('native GITHUB_TOKEN authenticates the GitHub Rulesets API fetch') and AC8 (.github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the workflow AC: 'permissions: administration: read' in the mirror-guard.yml job definition. Confirm by checking the GitHub REST API docs for GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets/{id} required permission.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `GITHUB_TOKEN` in a GitHub Actions workflow can read `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets` (confirm `administration: read` is or is not needed), and if so add `permissions: administration: read` to `mirror-guard.yml` and confirm the Actions app is allowed that permission for this repo.
findings.finding: The plan asserts GITHUB_TOKEN (the Actions-native token) suffices to authenticate the GitHub Rulesets API fetch (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets`), but the GitHub REST API requires `administration:read` (fine-grained PAT) or `repo` scope (classic token) to read repository rulesets. The default GITHUB_TOKEN in a GitHub Actions workflow is NOT granted `administration` permission by default — it would need an explicit `permissions: administration: read` block in the workflow, and that permission may need to be enabled at the repository Actions settings level. The existing codebase precedent (apply-mirror-lock.sh, ADR-0011, terraform-github/main.tf) consistently requires `Administration:write` for ruleset mutations but never exercises a read under GITHUB_TOKEN.
findings.location: Design section ('native GITHUB_TOKEN authenticates the GitHub Rulesets API fetch') and AC9 (.github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `permissions:\n  administration: read\n  contents: read` to the `mirror-guard.yml` workflow's top-level `permissions:` block, and confirm in the repo's Settings → Actions → General that the GITHUB_TOKEN is allowed `Read and write` or at minimum `Read` for Administration. Document this precondition explicitly in the AC or the workflow header comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare `permissions: administration: read` (plus any other minimum required scopes) in `.github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml` — the GitHub Rulesets API requires this scope which the default GITHUB_TOKEN does not carry; specify the exact permissions block in the plan's workflow AC.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'native GITHUB_TOKEN authenticates the GitHub Rulesets API fetch' with no further credential specification, but GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/rulesets/{id} requires the `administration` permission scope. The default GITHUB_TOKEN in GitHub Actions does NOT include `administration` permission unless the workflow explicitly declares `permissions: administration: read`. The plan's AC8/AC9 workflow definition does not mention this permissions block, leaving an ambiguous (and likely broken) access-level contract on the only authenticated boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: AC8: .github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml; Design section: 'native GITHUB_TOKEN authenticates the GitHub Rulesets API fetch'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the workflow YAML:

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific Gerrit REST endpoint and response field for the anonymous main SHA fetch (e.g. `GET /projects/{url-encoded-project}/branches/main` → `.revision`), confirm it is accessible without authentication on the production Gerrit instance, and document the `)]}'` prefix stripping requirement in the AC or fetcher spec.
findings.finding: The plan states the Gerrit main SHA is fetched via 'anon… public REST' but does not name the specific Gerrit REST endpoint (e.g. `/projects/{project}/branches/main`) or response field (`revision`). The codebase's existing Gerrit SHA reads all use `git ls-remote` over authenticated SSH (reviewbot-e2e.sh line 270), not the Gerrit REST API. It is unverified whether the production Gerrit instance at rebar.solutions.navateam.com exposes unauthenticated access to the branches REST endpoint, or whether the anonymous REST path (`/projects/…`) is even enabled.
findings.location: Design section ('the Gerrit main SHA is read ANONYMOUSLY (public REST)') and AC3 (src/rebar/mirror_guard.py thin I/O fetchers)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC11: explicitly state that a new bash section must be ADDED to `observability.sh` (it does not exist today) that fetches the Gerrit main SHA anonymously and the GitHub main SHA via `git ls-remote`, compares them, and publishes `rebar/host:mirror_out_of_sync`; also clarify how this relates to (and avoids duplicating) the existing `replication_errors` metric from S5.
findings.finding: AC11 asserts that `infra/scripts/observability.sh` 'publishes the rebar/host:mirror_out_of_sync metric via aws cloudwatch put-metric-data (dimensionless), comparing the anon Gerrit and GitHub main SHAs in bash' — but the actual observability.sh contains NO such logic. The file ends at line 119 with voter_errors; there is no `mirror_out_of_sync` metric, no `git ls-remote` call, and no SHA comparison anywhere in the file.
findings.location: AC11 / infra/scripts/observability.sh

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add lag-tolerance to `replication_verdict` so it does not treat a transient Gerrit-ahead-of-GitHub state as diverged — e.g. a configurable grace window (seconds since last known sync), a consecutive-evaluation threshold, or explicit documentation of how the caller de-noises the raw verdict before alerting.
findings.finding: The replication_verdict mechanism performs a single point-in-time SHA equality check on an inherently asynchronous replication system. After any Gerrit commit, there is a natural propagation window (replication.config: timeout=60s, up to 3 retries) during which Gerrit SHA > GitHub SHA is the correct transient state, not a failure. Without a lag tolerance (e.g., consecutive-periods threshold, a configurable grace window, or a minimum divergence duration), the scheduled watcher will produce persistent false-diverged verdicts during normal post-commit replication — alerting on healthy operation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (replication_verdict) / Design section / src/rebar/mirror_guard.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add an optional `max_lag_seconds` parameter to `replication_verdict`; the caller passes the time since the last known-healthy check (or the cron interval) and the function returns `in-sync` when the SHA difference is present but within the lag window. Alternatively, state in the plan that the CloudWatch alarm layer (not the Python verdict) handles de-noising via `evaluation_periods > 1`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit timeouts (e.g., `requests.get(..., timeout=10)` in Python fetchers; `timeout 15 git ls-remote ...` in bash) and surface fetch errors as an UNKNOWN/ERROR verdict or non-zero exit rather than hanging or silently skipping the check.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no timeout, retry, or error-handling for the external network calls in the Python fetchers (Gerrit anonymous REST, GitHub Rulesets API) or in the bash probe (git ls-remote, aws cloudwatch put-metric-data). If any of these endpoints is slow or unreachable, the call may hang or fail silently.
findings.location: Design section / src/rebar/mirror_guard.py (I/O fetchers) / infra/scripts/observability.sh
findings.suggested_fix: In mirror_guard.py fetchers, wrap requests calls with `timeout=(5, 10)` and catch `requests.exceptions.RequestException`, returning an error sentinel or raising a typed exception that the CLI prints as JSON `{"status": "error", "reason": "<msg>"}` and exits non-zero. In observability.sh, prefix git ls-remote with `timeout 15` and check exit code before invoking aws cloudwatch put-metric-data.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add test cases for each I/O fetcher covering at least: HTTP non-2xx response, network timeout, missing/empty response body, and absent/invalid GITHUB_TOKEN — and specify the expected CLI behavior (non-zero exit with a diagnostic message, not a bare Python traceback).
findings.finding: The test plan covers only the pure-logic verdict functions (replication_verdict, ruleset_verdict) with pre-fetched inputs, but specifies no tests for failure paths in the I/O fetchers (network timeout, HTTP error, auth failure, malformed/empty API response). For a monitoring system, a silently-failing fetcher could suppress alerts entirely — the exact failure mode the system is designed to catch.
findings.location: AC5, AC6 (behavioral tests) / src/rebar/mirror_guard.py fetcher functions
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Behavioral tests for each fetcher cover HTTP error (4xx/5xx), network timeout, and malformed response — each must cause the CLI to exit non-zero with a structured error message in the JSON output.' Also document in the AC for the CLI (AC4) that fetcher exceptions are caught and surfaced as a third verdict state (e.g. `error`) in the JSON output, so callers can distinguish fetch-failure from genuine health.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In monitoring_ws7.tf, set `treat_missing_data = "breaching"` on the CloudWatch alarm so that absent metric data is treated as out-of-sync (alarm state), ensuring the SNS alert fires when the bash probe cannot publish.
findings.finding: The CloudWatch alarm's missing-data treatment is unspecified. When the bash probe fails to run or fails to publish the metric, CloudWatch transitions the alarm to INSUFFICIENT_DATA (not ALARM), which typically does not trigger the SNS notification — meaning the alerting system silently fails when the probe itself is broken.
findings.location: infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf / infra/scripts/observability.sh
findings.suggested_fix: Add `treat_missing_data = "breaching"` to the aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm resource in monitoring_ws7.tf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that exercises the secondary CloudWatch path end-to-end: inject a simulated out-of-sync metric value and confirm the CloudWatch alarm transitions to ALARM state and the rebar-gerrit-alerts SNS topic receives a message.
findings.finding: The secondary CloudWatch alerting path (observability.sh publishes metric → Terraform alarm → SNS notification) is a new cutover code path with no acceptance criterion that exercises the full chain end-to-end in production. The plan has ACs for 'publishes the metric' and 'defines the alarm' but none that verify the alarm actually transitions to ALARM state and delivers an SNS notification when the metric signals out-of-sync.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'infra/scripts/observability.sh publishes the rebar/host:mirror_out_of_sync metric'; 'infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf defines a CloudWatch alarm on that metric wired to the rebar-gerrit-alerts SNS topic'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define distinct exit codes and/or a JSON `status` field (e.g., `healthy` / `unhealthy` / `probe_error`) for execution failures vs. genuine drift/lag, and add AC coverage for timeout and HTTP-error paths in the fetchers.
findings.finding: The plan does not distinguish between 'check executed and found a problem' (true unhealthy) and 'check could not execute' (network error, API timeout, HTTP 5xx from Gerrit/GitHub). Both would cause the CLI to exit non-zero and the workflow to fail, triggering an alert — but they require different responses (fix the mirror vs. fix the monitor). The AC only says 'exits non-zero when unhealthy' and 'workflow fails (producing a notification)', collapsing both states.
findings.location: AC: 'python -m rebar.mirror_guard runs both checks … exits non-zero when unhealthy' / AC: 'The workflow fails (producing a notification) when either check is unhealthy'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Fetcher functions raise a typed ProbeError on network/HTTP failure; the CLI catches it, emits `{"status": "probe_error", "detail": "..."}`, and exits with code 2 (distinct from exit 1 for unhealthy). Contractual tests pin exit code 2 for probe errors.' Also add a test case: fetcher raises on timeout → CLI output and exit code are correct.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan that `monitoring_ws7.tf` will reference `aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn` from `monitoring.tf` (not declare a new topic), and confirm this cross-file dependency is intentional and consistent with how `monitoring_s5.tf` does it.
findings.finding: infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf does not exist; the plan asserts it as a deliverable file (AC12, Files section) but no such file is present. Critically, the plan also asserts it will wire to the `rebar-gerrit-alerts` SNS topic — that topic is declared as `aws_sns_topic.alerts` in `monitoring.tf`, so the new file must reference a resource declared in a sibling file. The plan makes no assertion about this cross-file Terraform dependency.
findings.location: Files section / infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf; AC12
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: `alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]` — same pattern as monitoring_s5.tf and monitoring_s4b.tf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add monitoring.tf to the Files section as a READ dependency and note in AC11 that monitoring_ws7.tf should reference `aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn` (declared in monitoring.tf) rather than redeclaring an SNS topic.
findings.finding: infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf (new file) must reference aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn declared in infra/terraform/monitoring.tf to wire the CloudWatch alarm to the SNS topic per AC11, but monitoring.tf is not listed as a file the plan touches. This is an unenumerated consumer: the new tf file implicitly depends on a cross-file Terraform resource.
findings.location: AC11 / infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf
findings.suggested_fix: In AC11 add: '…wired to the rebar-gerrit-alerts SNS topic (`aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn` from monitoring.tf)…' and add `infra/terraform/monitoring.tf` as a read-only dependency in the Files section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior for each fetcher failure path: what JSON the CLI emits, which exit code it uses, and whether a fetch error counts as 'unhealthy' or as a distinct 'error' state — so AC7/AC8 contractual tests and AC9 workflow failure behavior are fully defined.
findings.finding: No fallback behavior is specified for the case where a fetcher sub-step (Gerrit SHA, GitHub SHA, or GitHub Ruleset) fails — the plan's processing protocol for the CLI and verdict functions covers only the happy-path (both SHAs available, ruleset fetchable), leaving the error-path output shape, exit code, and JSON structure undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3, AC4; Design section 'thin I/O fetchers'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's claim that monitoring_ws7.tf mirrors 'the S5 replication alarm' for SNS wiring — monitoring_s5.tf has no alarm_actions; instead, mirror the pattern from monitoring.tf (gerrit_gate_down alarm) which has `alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]`.
findings.finding: The plan states monitoring_ws7.tf should 'mirror the S5 replication alarm' for SNS wiring, but infra/terraform/monitoring_s5.tf contains NO alarm_actions or SNS topic reference at all — it is a standalone alarm with no notification wiring. Mirroring S5 will produce a silent alarm, not an alerting one.
findings.location: AC11 / Design section ('a Terraform alarm -> SNS') / infra/terraform/monitoring_s5.tf
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'mirroring the S5 replication alarm' in AC11 with 'following the monitoring.tf gerrit_gate_down pattern: `alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In `infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf`, explicitly specify the SNS wiring pattern: use a direct resource reference `aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn` (same Terraform root) for `alarm_actions`, and note that the S5/S4b files do NOT model this — monitoring.tf is the correct reference.
findings.finding: monitoring_ws7.tf's alarm_actions SNS wiring lacks a specified cross-file reference strategy: the plan says it mirrors the S5 replication alarm, but the actual infra/terraform/monitoring_s5.tf has NO alarm_actions line at all. The plan does not specify whether monitoring_ws7.tf will use a direct resource reference (`aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn`) or a data source lookup, and the stated mirror (S5) does not model the wiring — leaving the implementer without a grounded pattern and risking a silently unnotified alarm.
findings.location: AC: infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf — 'wired to the rebar-gerrit-alerts SNS topic'; infra/terraform/monitoring_s5.tf (no alarm_actions present)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]` and `ok_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]` to the alarm resource in monitoring_ws7.tf, matching the pattern in `infra/terraform/monitoring.tf` lines 88/135/161. Explicitly note in the plan (or file header) that monitoring_s5.tf is NOT the correct model for wiring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (or provide an example of) the JSON output schema for the CLI — at minimum specify the top-level keys and the representation of healthy vs. unhealthy state for each check.
findings.finding: The JSON output schema for the CLI ('prints a JSON result') is referenced but never defined in the plan. 'Contractual tests pin the verdict output schema' implies the schema exists, but an executor must invent it — keys, value types, and the representation of healthy/unhealthy states are all unspecified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'python -m rebar.mirror_guard runs both checks, prints a JSON result'; 'Contractual tests pin the verdict output schema'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an example: `{"replication": {"status": "in-sync"|"diverged", "gerrit_sha": "...", "github_sha": "..."}, "ruleset": {"status": "locked"|"drifted", "violations": [...]}}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the explicit verdict value vocabulary for both `replication_verdict` (e.g. enum `'in_sync' | 'diverged'`) and `ruleset_verdict` (e.g. `'locked' | 'disabled' | 'wrong_ref' | 'missing_rule' | 'extra_bypass' | 'deleted'`) in the plan, and reference or sketch the JSON output schema the CLI emits so AC7's contractual tests have something concrete to pin.
findings.finding: The verdict value vocabulary for replication_verdict ('in-sync/diverged') and ruleset_verdict ('locked' vs each drift state) is referenced throughout the ACs but never enumerated as an explicit enum or defined type. An implementer cannot derive the actual string/object literals from the plan alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, AC2, AC5, AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Schema' subsection to the plan defining: (a) `replication_verdict` returns a string literal from `Literal['in_sync', 'diverged']`; (b) `ruleset_verdict` returns a string from `Literal['locked', 'disabled', 'wrong_ref', 'missing_rule', 'extra_bypass', 'deleted']`; (c) the CLI JSON shape, e.g. `{"replication": "in_sync", "ruleset": "locked", "healthy": true}`. Reference this from AC7.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the CLI output schema (field names, types, allowed values) and the full exit-code table (0=healthy, 1=unhealthy, 2=fetch/config error, etc.) to the plan so AC7 and AC8 contractual tests have a concrete, reviewable specification to pin.
findings.finding: The CLI's JSON output schema and exit-code contract are referenced as things that 'contractual tests pin' (AC7, AC8) but the schema itself is never defined in the plan — the plan is self-referentially incomplete: it promises tests that pin a schema that does not yet exist anywhere.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4, AC7, AC8

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the exact GitHub Rulesets API representation of the DeployKey bypass in AC2 (or a linked spec): `actor_type='DeployKey'`, `bypass_mode='always'`, `actor_id` absent/null — so `ruleset_verdict` implementers and test authors have a precise predicate to code against.
findings.finding: The `ruleset_verdict` decision rule for 'bypass = DeployKey only' depends on a specific GitHub Rulesets API representation (actor_type='DeployKey', bypass_mode='always', no actor_id) that is documented only in infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md — it is never stated in the plan, leaving the check's operationalization undefined for an executor reading only the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'bypass = DeployKey only'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 to read: '...bypass = exactly one DeployKey actor (`actor_type: "DeployKey"`, `bypass_mode: "always"`, no `actor_id`), matching infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md §DeployKey bypass actor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `mirror_out_of_sync` (SHA-comparison) and `replication_errors` (log-error-count) are intentionally complementary with distinct alert semantics, or whether the plan is inadvertently rebuilding existing S5 alarm coverage — if complementary, add a brief justification in monitoring_ws7.tf explaining how the two alarms differ (e.g. 'replication_errors catches push rejections; mirror_out_of_sync catches silent drift where no error is logged').
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `rebar/host:mirror_out_of_sync` CloudWatch metric (observability.sh section) + `monitoring_ws7.tf` alarm for replication failure, but this overlaps directly with the already-live `rebar/host:replication_errors` metric published by the existing S5 section of observability.sh and alarmed by monitoring_s5.tf — rebuilding replication-health coverage that already exists in the codebase.
findings.location: AC for infra/scripts/observability.sh (mirror_out_of_sync metric) and infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf; Design section ('SECONDARY, house-style replication watcher')
findings.suggested_fix: If the SHA-comparison metric is intended to catch divergences that produce no replication_log error (e.g. a Gerrit rollback rather than a push failure), document that distinction explicitly in both the plan and the TF file. If it truly duplicates S5, extend monitoring_s5.tf with the SNS action instead of creating a parallel alarm.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale in the Design section explaining what failure mode `mirror_out_of_sync` (SHA comparison) catches that `replication_errors` (log patterns) does NOT — e.g. 'silent-success divergence where replication completes without log errors but history has diverged' — and confirm the two alarms are intentionally complementary rather than duplicates.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `mirror_out_of_sync` CloudWatch metric and `monitoring_ws7.tf` alarm that detect the same failure mode (Gerrit→GitHub replication divergence) as the already-live `replication_errors` metric and `monitoring_s5.tf` alarm, but provides no positive rationale for why a second, parallel alarm signal is needed. Without a stated rationale, an implementer cannot determine whether these two signals are intentionally complementary (e.g. SHA-compare catches silent-success divergences not reflected in error logs) or accidental duplication that will produce split-brain alerting.
findings.location: Design section ('SECONDARY, house-style replication watcher') / AC10-AC11 / infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly justify `rebar/host:mirror_out_of_sync` (SHA-comparison) as complementary to the existing `rebar/host:replication_errors` (log-grep) alarm — or consolidate them — documenting what failure class each catches that the other misses (e.g., log-rotation gaps, replication appearing healthy in logs but SHA still diverged).
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new `rebar/host:mirror_out_of_sync` CloudWatch metric + alarm (observability.sh + monitoring_ws7.tf) that is semantically redundant with the already-live `rebar/host:replication_errors` metric + alarm in monitoring_s5.tf, which already detects Gerrit→GitHub replication failures. No justification for a second replication-failure signal is given.
findings.location: Design section ('SECONDARY, house-style replication watcher') and AC10/AC11 (infra/scripts/observability.sh, infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a design note: 'replication_errors detects active replication failures logged by the plugin; mirror_out_of_sync detects the resulting state (SHA divergence) even if no new log lines are produced (e.g., after log rotation or a silent partial failure). These are complementary signals.' If that distinction does not hold, remove monitoring_ws7.tf and extend monitoring_s5.tf instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact GitHub Rulesets API actor representation for the DeployKey bypass (e.g. `actor_type: 'DeployKey'`, `actor_id: 0`) so the executor can implement `ruleset_verdict` without investigation.
findings.finding: The 'bypass = DeployKey only' constraint in ruleset_verdict is not mapped to a specific GitHub Rulesets API actor representation (actor_type, actor_id, or named value). An executor cannot implement or test this condition without knowing the exact API field names and values — this is not inferrable from the plan or a universal convention.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'src/rebar/mirror_guard.py exposes ruleset_verdict(ruleset) enforcing the full lock contract … bypass = DeployKey only'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC or Design section: 'bypass actor must satisfy actor_type == "DeployKey" (actor_id is ignored / must be 0 per GitHub's deploy-key actor convention)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `concurrency:` group (e.g., `group: mirror-guard` with `cancel-in-progress: true` or `false` depending on desired semantics) to the workflow to prevent overlapping cron and manual-dispatch runs.
findings.finding: The workflow definition (.github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml) has no specified concurrency group, so a manual dispatch can overlap a cron-triggered run. Both instances will independently call the GitHub Rulesets API and Gerrit REST endpoint, and if either reports unhealthy, two independent failure notifications will fire — doubling alerts for a single real event and potentially confusing incident response.
findings.location: .github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml / AC: 'runs the CLI on a cron schedule plus manual dispatch'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the workflow top-level:
```yaml
concurrency:
  group: mirror-guard
  cancel-in-progress: false  # queue rather than cancel so no run is silently dropped
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference `infra/gerrit/reviewbot-e2e.sh` (lines 270–293) as prior art for the git ls-remote SHA-comparison approach and `src/rebar/review_bot/gerrit_client.py` as the established urllib/Gerrit pattern, and either reuse them or explicitly justify why a new implementation is needed (e.g., anonymous access vs authenticated, stdlib-only constraint).
findings.finding: The plan's 'thin I/O fetchers' for the anonymous Gerrit main SHA and GitHub main SHA reinvent a `git ls-remote`-based SHA comparison that already exists in `infra/gerrit/reviewbot-e2e.sh` (lines 270–293), and the Gerrit HTTP fetcher duplicates the urllib pattern already established in `src/rebar/review_bot/gerrit_client.py`. Neither prior-art source is referenced.
findings.location: AC3 ('provides thin I/O fetchers for the Gerrit main SHA (anon), the GitHub main SHA') and src/rebar/mirror_guard.py
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Design section, add: 'The Gerrit anon SHA fetcher uses the same git ls-remote idiom as reviewbot-e2e.sh; the GitHub ruleset fetcher follows the urllib pattern in gerrit_client.py. mirror_guard.py is stdlib-only to avoid adding dependencies, consistent with that module.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the lock contract parameters (required rules, allowed bypass actors, target ref pattern) into a named constant block or small config structure in mirror_guard.py so they can be updated without restructuring the verdict logic.
findings.finding: The ruleset lock contract enforced by ruleset_verdict is fully hardcoded in Python logic (active, target=branch, refs/heads/main, rules: update+deletion+non_fast_forward, bypass=DeployKey only) with no mention of making it configurable. This contract is likely to evolve (new bypass actors, additional protected refs, rule additions) and will require code changes rather than configuration updates.
findings.location: AC: 'src/rebar/mirror_guard.py exposes ruleset_verdict(ruleset) enforcing the full lock contract (active, target=branch, refs/heads/main, rules update+deletion+non_fast_forward, bypass = DeployKey only)'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a `LOCK_CONTRACT = { 'target_type': 'branch', 'target_pattern': 'refs/heads/main', 'required_rules': {'update', 'deletion', 'non_fast_forward'}, 'allowed_bypasses': ['DeployKey'] }` dict at module level and have `ruleset_verdict` validate against it, making the contract a single source of truth.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite the live-drift AC as a durable, re-runnable check: e.g. 'Running the guardian CLI against a repository with a known ruleset mutation exits non-zero and names the violation in its JSON output' (achievable via a fixture repo or a scripted drift-and-detect sequence treated as a single atomic test).
findings.finding: The AC 'A simulated ruleset drift is detected by the guardian, then reverted' conflates a detection proof (durable) with a restoration action ('then reverted') that is a one-time transition. After reversion the state is identical to before, so the AC cannot be re-evaluated as true — it describes a procedure, not a durable end-state.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'A simulated ruleset drift is detected by the guardian, then reverted'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a docs/adr/ entry (or CLAUDE.md section) documenting the two-watcher decision: why both exist, which failure modes each covers, and what action each alert demands — and add it to the Files list.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel two-watcher architecture (Python CLI + GH Actions as primary; bash + CloudWatch + SNS as secondary) for the same concern, but does not capture this architectural decision — the rationale for two watchers, their differing failure modes, escalation semantics, and how they relate — in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc.
findings.location: Design section ('two independent, secretless watchers') / Files list

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `tests/unit/test_mirror_guard.py` requires no new conftest.py fixtures or pytest configuration changes (or call out what must be added).
findings.finding: tests/unit/test_mirror_guard.py does not exist; the plan asserts it as a deliverable file but provides no assertion about the test infrastructure it will slot into (e.g. conftest.py fixtures, pytest configuration, import path). This is a net-new file that must be grounded.
findings.location: Files section / tests/unit/test_mirror_guard.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge that `.github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml` is a fully new workflow file and specify its trigger syntax (`schedule` cron + `workflow_dispatch`) and permissions block explicitly in the plan.
findings.finding: .github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml does not exist; the plan asserts it as a deliverable but the existing workflows directory contains no file by that name, so there is no basis to claim any structure is inherited or reused.
findings.location: Files section / .github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly label src/rebar/mirror_guard.py, tests/unit/test_mirror_guard.py, .github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml, and infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf as NEW FILES (CREATION) in the plan's Files section, since none of them currently exist in the repo.
findings.finding: src/rebar/mirror_guard.py, tests/unit/test_mirror_guard.py, .github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml, and infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf are all absent from the repo — they are net-new files, not edits to existing targets. The plan's AC language treats them as concrete existing paths, but implementers must CREATE them from scratch.
findings.location: Files section / AC1-AC12
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Files section to: `NEW: src/rebar/mirror_guard.py, tests/unit/test_mirror_guard.py, .github/workflows/mirror-guard.yml, infra/terraform/monitoring_ws7.tf | AMENDED: infra/scripts/observability.sh`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `python -m rebar.mirror_guard` works without changes to `src/rebar/__main__.py`: confirm `mirror_guard.py` will contain an `if __name__ == '__main__'` block (or explicitly state that `__main__.py` will be updated to delegate).
findings.finding: src/rebar/mirror_guard.py does not exist in the codebase; the plan treats it as a file to be created but the 'Files' section and multiple ACs reference it as if it will simply be added — no assertion of prior existence is made, which is correct — however this must be flagged as a net-new file with no prior hook to any existing module, meaning the plan makes no assertion about integration points (e.g. how __main__.py or the package __init__.py surface it).
findings.location: Files section / src/rebar/mirror_guard.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add `if __name__ == '__main__': sys.exit(main())` to mirror_guard.py and note this explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Design how observability.sh obtains AWS credentials for `aws cloudwatch put-metric-data` (e.g., EC2 instance profile, GitHub Actions OIDC role, or pre-existing IAM credential) and reconcile with the 'secretless / no new secret' claim.
findings.finding: The Design header claims 'two independent, secretless watchers' and 'no new secret,' but observability.sh must call 'aws cloudwatch put-metric-data,' which requires AWS credentials (IAM role or key). The plan never explains how the bash probe is authenticated to AWS, creating an ambiguity between the 'secretless' framing and the actual auth requirement.
findings.location: Design section ('no new secret') and AC: 'infra/scripts/observability.sh publishes the rebar/host:mirror_out_of_sync metric via aws cloudwatch put-metric-data'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Design: 'observability.sh authenticates to CloudWatch via the existing GitHub Actions OIDC-to-IAM role (no new long-lived secret); this is what 'no new secret' means — the AWS auth re-uses the already-provisioned OIDC trust.' If no OIDC role exists, replace 'secretless' with 'no new GitHub secret' and note the required AWS credential setup.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
